speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five with Irving Eisner. Mr. Eisner, you were telling me that Dr. Weiss had called you to help him. Please, continue the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I asked him, what-- what do you mean, carry or-- or walk behind? He says to me, you know. He said, I brought you from the other world. You were 99 and 1/2 percent death when you have the typhus. So I said, yes, Doctor, so? I want you to pray to God. That because of you-- I save you, you shall save me. And this Dr. Weiss was not a religious Jew at all. I said, Doctor, I will pray. That is what I can  do, only pray. And they took him. I didn't see him anymore, because he revived quicker than I did. They took him into a  labor camp, because the war came almost to an end, and they put him into a torturing camp. I didn't see him. In 1944, when I came-- 1945, sorry. When I came home, I found my father. I went down to the market. My father gave me money. And I walked, for the market was a main street. I'm eating some berries or something, which I bought. And all of a sudden, I see Dr. Weiss walking with two beautiful ladies on the main road. I didn't know what to do. I was approaching him, face to face, and he claps his hands. In Hungarian-- [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN] --he is my child from the other world. The ladies are looking at him. One happens to turn to be his wife-- the second. He lost his wife. And the other girl was a sister of that woman that he is courting now. And-- [SIGHS] but I don't have to tell you what happened when we met. And I tell him, Dr. Weiss, you know, my father is alive. My father used to be his best friend. Used to play cards once a week before the war. I said to him, come to my place. We found a house. Let's drink l'chaim that we are alive. He says, I don't drink l'chaim. I don't want to make a bracha. What he has done to us, you know it, he tells me. And I shall say a bracha.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say to him, but Doctor, we are here. Yes, he did wrong things. We don't know-- he-- he tortured, he did  a lot. But we are here! Let's drink l'chaim! OK, I will go just because of your father, but I don't make a bracha. [LAUGHS] I said, we will see to it. So he came to our house, and we drank l'chaim. I don't remember if we made a bracha or not. I don't remember. So he said to my father, I brought your son. He will tell you. Let them go with me to America. I will let him study. I will make him  for a doctor. I will pay, because he was my student once. Let him go with me to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, my father-- I was only one boy, and then-- then my sister came. Naturally, he didn't want. And finally, he went to the States, and he opened old folks home, and he became the doctor in Long Island. That's what I heard. And when my son, Nate, became bar mitzvah, I invited him. I got the address. And we had a packed house to my first son's bar mitzvah. And he came with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stood up, and I said, this is a man. Go to him. If not for him, this bar mitzvah wouldn't have been. Everybody was silent, like the rock. And he stood up, everybody applauded. And then he went back and he-- he left, you know? He just died from old age. That's all. So things are happening. In the meantime, we've gone--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ahead in our story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with your permission, we can go back to Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to help Dr. Weiss, did you leave the lager at Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there. The only difference was like a ditch in between. In between--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Dr. Weiss and myself was a [NON-ENGLISH], and there was a typhuslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- and Dr. Weiss was taken away, because the war came to-- almost to an end. And they didn't care, doctor, or not doctor-- a Jew. They took them away to a hard labor camp. And I was left in the sick people's camp. And I knew, again, this is a matter of days, I'm going to die. And here, the war came almost to  an end. And then they came with the order that everybody who can walk should-- is going to leave tomorrow morning the camp, be taken to Dachau, back to Dachau. It turned out to be Allach, because Dachau was filled up with people. Because-- and those who cannot walk should stay. They will bring wagons with horses, or trucks. They will take them those few kilometers. It wasn't very far from-- from-- from Dachau, or Allach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, again, [NON-ENGLISH]. What shall I do? But my sense told me, the Germans are not going to take people with wagons and with horses or with trucks. They wouldn't do it. They would just kill them. But I still wanted to stay, because I was very, very weak to walk. I knew I can't make it. So what happened-- there was a gate to go out of the camp. Those who want to march should go outside of the gate. I don't what to do. But I notice that they're giving bread. A loaf-- a whole loaf of bread for those who are going to walk and a whole package of margarine. So when I saw the bread, I said, I'm going to go over there. First let's have the bread. I went over the gate. I took the bread with the margarine. And I'm asking people older than I am, friends of my father-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I can't give you an advice. I don't know myself what to do-- to stay or to go. And I decided, with the bread and with the margarine, to go back. I knew it's not good. And as I approached the gate, they locked down the gate. I couldn't make it back. And I stood with those who are marching. And I had to go to march. And again, I was good to people. I helped people, and people helped me a lot too. People, strangers, I don't know who they are-- Jewish people. They saw I'm struggling. I can't walk too fast. They help me. And I was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea what the fates of-- of the people-- I do. --who remained behind was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I  do, because in fact, upstairs, I have a little-- about 12 pages. It just so happens, a young man who [? davens ?] with-- prays with me in my synagogue, he read my book. And he read about Kaufering and Landsberg. He came rushing to me. He says, [NON-ENGLISH]. My father [? were ?] there. And he went to Washington, to the memorial museum, and he brought me pictures-- this young man. I asked him now to make copies for me, because I heard the Germans went there next morning, and they poured gasoline or [NON-ENGLISH] all over the barracks, and they burned the camp. That's what I heard, but I wasn't sure. He brought me now pictures from Washington to show me the camp-- how the camp was burning, so I know what happened to the camp. And his father was burned there. He didn't want to walk. So I know what happened to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken on this death march from Kaufering.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Kaufering, we went to Dachau. We arrived in Dachau at night, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of them were-- were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not many, just several hundred, about 400 or 500. The rest died already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans, the SS, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, already at that time, they were lose. They were scared of their own life. They were just leading us, but they were scared. Only old, old people came with us. You know, usually, were old people, or young people, teenagers. And they-- in fact, it was  a day-- they were hiding between us, because Americans came so low with their airplanes, they could tell who were German and who was a prisoner. So the Germans used to hide between us, not to be noticed by the American snipers. So they didn't look so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we had to go. Where are we  going to go? We came to Dachau, and we-- we were put in barracks. They were so filled up, but we were walking on people-- living people, still-- half dead. I remember, I came in, I left a little bag. I go to the washroom. I came back, I never could find it. I did not know where I was. So we were walking on people. It was so filled up with-- with bodies-- living still. They were still alive. So the same night, they started to yell, we're going further, another five kilometers, to Allach. It's beside Dachau. So we went to Allach. And there, again, we were left almost without-- without Germans, because they knew the war here is-- is ending. And you knew then it was ending?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only heard the shootings, the bombardments of Munich, because we are right beside Munich. Allach is just beside Munich. So-- and one morning-- I even don't know today how-- it was-- one more day or two, I wouldn't have been survived, because it was the end. We had not what to eat, what to do drink. We're all just-- I couldn't walk anymore. I was just climbing on my legs and my hands, and-- And we were sleeping, sleeping. And one morning, we woke-- and I heard the whole night of-- last night was terrible bombardments and shooting. Even our barracks was-- we were-- we were shaking. And one morning, I woke up, early morning, and I start-- I go out, and I see that the towers of the SS are empty, and little white rags, [NON-ENGLISH] are hanging. They surrendered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say to my friend, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, we can go out. He didn't-- he couldn't move, he was so-- when I climbed-- I climbed, I didn't have fear anymore. So the first thing was-- I went to the German military camp. There was no Germans anymore. I-- I wasn't afraid. I climbed in. The first thing-- I was smelling, where can I get some food? I'm dying! So I smelled and smelled some potato soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went by the smell into a kitchen-- a German kitchen, and I found a big bowl still warm soup. They left at night, the Germans. I put in my head and I was just eating soup. Thanks God, I didn't kill myself. And then I said, I'm going to go back, but I'm filled with lice. I'm dirty. I have to change my clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, I-- I found the barracks. I opened up a cupboard, and I found SS new clothes that put me [INAUDIBLE], later. The Germans-- the Russians almost killed me. They think I'm Russian. So I threw out all my lice dirty clothes. I put on new ones-- under-- underwear, and everything. First time I have underwear, clean ones. And I put on a uniform, a German uniform, it was clean. And a pair of shoes. And I marched back slowly, slowly to the camp. I marched back to the camp. And what happened in the camp? The Americans came in. First came a soldier in from the highway. He happened to be a Jewish guy. He came in. And I was so lucky. I even have pictures somewhere. He found me. He started to make pictures. Turn here, turn around. And I didn't know where-- I was thinking that-- I didn't know a Jew was still alive at that time. So he says to me-- [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Don't be afraid, I'm a Jew. I said, what? So chocolate, he gave me. And then he took his pistol, he started to shoot in the air. And a whole caravan, you know, from the highway, through the fields, started to come into our camp. And they started to take-- but they made a terrible mistake. They put up kitchens, and they-- they began to give us fat food, you know, the meat, beef. And people became sick of the stomachs. And they started to die. Then they stopped it. So-- but I, being also cautious, I begin to think about kosher. Who cared so much? So I found a man, Mr. Meyerowicz, also the age of my father. And we were very good related. And I say to him-- I knew he was a very orthodox man. I said, Mr. Meyerowicz, we don't eat that food, not because I was afraid it's damaging our stomach. I said, we're going to [NON-ENGLISH] food from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will go bring in some potatoes from the German camp, a little bit of wood. We have matches. We'll make a fire. You will be the cook, I'll be the supplier. And we ate. We ate the plate. And you know, after being a few days, you have-- you lost the fear. You're not afraid of anybody. I said to Mr. Meyerowicz, what happened, a tragedy. He had two sons, 20 and 22. They went into Munich. They couldn't make it to the city. It was over 15 kilometers, about. And they over-ate. They never returned. They died after the war from the food what they ate in Munich. The father comes to me, fainting, because Mayer and Yossel didn't return. And he has the diarrhea. The father-- was 45, 46-years-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I made myself ready a bed, because I found also from Munkacs a doctor-- a different doctor. He took care of sick people. So I went in. I  said, Dr. Spiegel, can I stay in your barracks? He says, make yourself a bed. There was three beds, one, two, three. I made myself a bed on the bottom. And I said Mr. Meyerowicz, you take my bed. I will go up above. And I'm going in to the next village beside the camp. I'll bring some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into to a village-- I forgot what it was called-- and I was not afraid. I went to the first farmer, to his kitchen. And you would think that, if you would make [BLOWS AIR] like this, I would fall down. And the German sitting there, a goy, a non-Jew, a civilian. I go to his kitchen, I take oil from-- oil, to cook. I take potatoes. I go to his back yard, I take out of the chicken coop a chicken, and I kill it. [LAUGHS] I was afraid to kill that chicken. Put it on my back. And I take some onions, and he doesn't say a word. And I march back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you weigh at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home, I was 40 kilos. My father picked me up like this. On the very home-- what Americas did, it was so nice, but they made mistakes. [LAUGHS] Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, when were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war was over. The day the war was over, May the 8th-- something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 1945. And on the way home, you know, the Americans they did? They set up tables in the fields. And everyone who was walking, they called them to sit down beside the tables-- like a [NON-ENGLISH] table. They served food. And people became sick. They meant well. So on the way to the village, they called me too. And I said, no, no, no. I'm going first to get some food for Mr. Meyerowicz. On the way back, I couldn't resist the temptation. I had a bag with me-- a chicken, some-- some potatoes, some-- some onions, big. They're calling, so I said, I have to go. [LAUGHS] And I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to me? I ate. I ate too much. So walking from there, another three kilometers, oh, oh, I felt that's it. I couldn't-- excuse me-- keep my pants on. I had to run. And-- but I had to go back. I can't-- I'm going to die on the road somewhere here. So slowly, slowly, I made it to the camp, and I came to the entrance. Again, two angels from the heaven-- two [NON-ENGLISH] Jews-- I never saw them in my life before-- they were the first two people I saw at the gate. I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Here is my bag. Take it. I can't smell it. I can't look at it. But please, save me. I'm dying. They knew right away what happened, that I ate too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those two Jews, may they be [NON-ENGLISH], they took me, they put me down to sleep beside them on the floor. And they gave me roasted bread, a little bit of tea. And they brought me back to life. The next day, I went, I found Meyerowicz. I took him to the washrooms. I cleaned him, and I brought him home to Chust.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want-- before we talk about your liberation, you mentioned in your book, you scheduled the struggle to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: How was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You scheduled the struggle to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means, what I believe-- some people do not agree. My belief is, if people would have tried harder to apply some ways of struggling to stay alive, my opinion is that, at least, close to a million would have been survived. What I mean is the following. For example, in the camp, the sick people's camp-- I begged my friend, the same age, let's go out to fresh air. I will help you. He said no. We are warm here, under the blanket, and the lice ate them up-- the lice. There were camps that was made [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is to get rid of lice. It was hard to go there, because it was very hot inside. It was steam. You had-- you had to undress, pull your clothes into a little package. You sort them to an oven. They came out the other side. But the lice were burned. So you take back your clothes and put it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to wait-- to wait there, naked until the clothes come out. It was very, very hot. And some people didn't want-- maybe they're hot. Who knows what? There were cases people had to go. They didn't want. I went for them, and they gave me a piece of bread for that. I went not once, but twice in the same month to [NON-ENGLISH]. So what I want to say, if people would have to tried, like I did, to try to find something to eat, to try-- to-- to-- to struggle more, to-- to clean yourself a little bit more, not to give up so easy, many would have been survived. It was a struggle. I said before, it was very easy to die. To live is very hard. And I did not think for a moment that I will survive to come home. The only thing was, I wanted to live for another hour, another day, maybe another week. But I struggled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, what I said-- once I mentioned, my nourishment was also not just the food. I brought with me some nourishment-- spiritual. Spiritual, because I knew by heart some Jewish songs, some Hebrew songs. And no matter how hard it was, I was murmuring to myself, I was singing to myself. A Jew can cry, even without tears. I just said this to [NON-ENGLISH] in our shul. When Moshe [NON-ENGLISH] was a baby, he was thrown into the waters-- you know the story. And the daughter of Pharaoh saw him. She opened, and she saw a child crying. [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The question is, if a child cries, you hear, you don't see. So why is it she saw a baby crying? She saw his crying. She saw the tears. She didn't-- he wasn't crying loud. The tears, he was struggling to stay alive. Only a Jew can cry without tears. Egyptian cannot cry without tears. That's when she saw it's a Jewish child. Only a Jewish child can cry without tears-- a struggle. And this is what people didn't-- and I brought this with me from home, many of those things. And I said, I depend on The Creator. Yes, yes, not, not. But what else is left? Without hope at all, it's very easy to give up. So this was my struggling. That's why I said, you have to-- there was a way to struggle. If they struggle, maybe more would have-- I-- my opinion is, many would have been survived. But they did not struggle too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many did. I don't want, God forbid, to tell that people didn't struggle, didn't try. That's not my view. But I say, not enough people tried, and not strong enough they try-- they tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you put it very beautifully in your book. 'One does not love when one pleads to love.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'One does not die--

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when one wants to die.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. It's not our choice. Sometimes, you know, a person can be strong, like-- like-- a [NON-ENGLISH], especially like an ox, and sometimes weaker, like a fly. Is-- we don't know how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: If somebody would come to  tell me now, today, as you are sitting here, that you go to work in December-- it's freezing outside-- in garments which are thin, like paper. On the way, or back from work, or doing the work, there comes a rain that's frost. All your garments are frozen like a sheet of metal on your body. Right? You go to sleep in the same garments. And tomorrow morning, you go back to work, day after day, in [INAUDIBLE] your life. Now, is that possible? No colds. No fever. What is this? It's hard to believe. I wouldn't believe it. It would be hard for me to believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I am here to tell you, to tell you exactly what I went through, and many like me. Naturally, everybody has his own story. There's no end to it. I'm just telling daily life in the camps. And my first book, really, what I wrote, of daily life, struggle and survival, how I survived, how my father-- my father [INAUDIBLE] survived. My second book, I wrote last year only, when I was privileged by the Jewish Congress of Toronto. They heard about me, because I'm teaching. They know me. They asked me if I would go with the March of the Living students from Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to get to that a little bit later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were talking, before we digressed a bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Go back, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, May, 1945, what did you want to do? You wanted to return home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell-- yes. What happened was, we were freed by the Americans, as I mentioned before. They first came, as I said, the young people, at least till 20 or so, they will take them to the States, to America. But those who went-- we had to register, matter of  fact-- those who want to go home, or to go to a lot of places, or go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I decided I want to go home. I was almost positive I wouldn't find my father, no one. But I remembered that my uncle-- I spoke about him before, Avraham Katz-- he was a soldier. I figured maybe he survived. So in fact, when they asked me, with whom do you want to go? I did not say my father or my father's address, I said to my uncle. So I went home. But they gave us a chance to go wherever we want. And also, they sent us to recovering places for a week, two, three, four, as long as we want, to different places in Czechoslovakia or in Germany, for their own expenses. They did whatever they could, the Americans, to-- to-- to help us out. So I decided to go home. And I went home. I went home, back to Chust, to see what-- and luckily, I found my father. And later on, my sister came home. Describe for us that reunion with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it sweet? Was it bitter? Was it bittersweet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bittersweet. Bittersweet. Very bittersweet. It was bitter, because we lost the family. It was bitter, because we lost 6 million Jews. It was bitter, because we turned to be [? worse ?] than animals. We, who were once considered beautiful-- I mean, nice people, ordinary people, help for others. And now, we turned into garbage, into nothing. It was very bitter to come home and to find empty the house, empty the city, empty the village. But it was sweet from the other side, that I found my father. And when I came to the station-- my father was the first Jew to be in Chust, in January, in 1945, because he escaped the third time. And he opened his house. We had a large house on my name. He bought it to me for my bar mitzvah. And everyone came the train. We went twice in the morning and the evening to wait for people. They all were taken to this house. He supplied the food. There were ladies who cooked. The rest were eating. Even marriages, we-- my father arranged in the city of Chust. And he waited, he waited, he waited. And ever who arrived-- in fact, one person who made business with my father every day, and he saw me just before liberation. He told my father, don't expect your son. We saw him. He was a skeleton-- called a Muselmann. Father was crying. And when I arrived-- I walked. I had with me a pair of new shoes to carrying, and in my SS clothes. That's how I arrived. And my cousin noticed me before, ahead. The train-- before the train stopped, he jumped to tell my father. And my uncle, [NON-ENGLISH], was holding me on the train, because they made business-- they took the train on a station before we arrived to Chust. So he was holding me. And he was running to tell my father. [POINTS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I went down. I went to my father. [CRYING] He picked me up. He says, are you my child? Are you my child? He wanted to carry me. I said, tate, [NON-ENGLISH] I can walk. And all people from the house, women and men, were there. And they all went, God forbid, like [NON-ENGLISH]. That's because it was a [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me to the house. They put me in to sleep, right away, to the-- I couldn't sleep in a bed. I had to sleep on the floor [LAUGHS] for a few nights. Every day, a few hours on the bed. I couldn't get used to softness. And I was 40 kilo when he picked me up, because he took me to a doctor. They measured, and they put me on the weight. And the doctor said-- I came with-- I went away with big, beautiful, curly hair. You saw my pictures. You can see it. I came back-- one piece of hair here, piece of hair there. And they decided I must eat pork. [NON-ENGLISH]. The doctor-- I went to a doctor, he said I must eat, for a few weeks, pork. So I went-- my father went to a new rabbi, who was also a survivor. He said, yes, I'm allowed to. It was the doctor says. But we decided not. It will take a little bit longer, but I will survive. The hair came back to me. And that's how it was when I came home.
